results.expected_behavior: Suspicious or Likely scam. It must identify the untrusted payment link, urgency, payment demand, and threat.

candidate.simple_explanation: This message claims to be from Pakistan Post but uses a fake URL and demands immediate payment to prevent destruction of a parcel.
red_flags: Fake sender name 'PAKISTAN POST' used in a suspicious context
Request for immediate payment via an untrusted link
Threat of parcel destruction to force quick action
URL domain 'pakpost-delivery.example' is not an official Pakistan Post domain
safe_next_steps: Do not click the link or make any payment.
Contact Pakistan Post directly using their official website or customer care number found on their verified social media.
Check your official courier account or statement for any legitimate return notices.
Report the message to your mobile carrier or the National Anti-Fraud Centre.

judgment.reason: The candidate correctly identifies the message as a 'Likely scam'. It accurately grounds its claims in the provided text, specifically citing the fake URL, the demand for immediate payment, and the threat of destruction. The safe next steps explicitly advise against clicking the link or paying, and suggest using official channels instead. All required fields are present, useful, and internally consistent.

results.expected_behavior: Suspicious or Likely scam. The screenshot shows a failed-delivery parcel message with a clickable rearrangement link. Branding alone must not be treated as proof of legitimacy.

candidate.simple_explanation: This message mimics a delivery update but uses a generic sender name and a clickable link, which are common indicators of a phishing attempt.
red_flags: Sender name is generic ('POST OFFICE') rather than a specific local post office.
Contains a clickable link ('Click here') which is a primary vector for phishing.
Message claims a parcel was returned to a depot, a common pretext for scams.
Sender icon is a generic placeholder, not the official Pakistan Post logo.
safe_next_steps: Do not click the link in the message.
Visit the official Pakistan Post website directly by typing the URL into your browser.
Contact your local post office using a known phone number to verify the status of your parcel.
Report the message to your mobile carrier as a potential scam.
candidate.reply_draft: Do not click the link. Verify the parcel status by contacting your local post office directly.

judgment.reason: The candidate correctly identifies the message as suspicious, accurately citing visible evidence such as the generic sender name, the clickable link, and the lack of official branding. The recommended steps are safe and avoid engaging with the suspicious content.

results.expected_behavior: Suspicious or Likely scam. It offers an iPhone/gifts and redirects the recipient to a visible WhatsApp phone number.

candidate.simple_explanation: This message claims you won a prize and asks you to contact a new WhatsApp number, which is a common tactic in advance-fee scams.
red_flags: Claim of winning a prize (14 promax, gifts)
Request to contact via a new WhatsApp number
Untrusted sender number (0349 7729067)
No official verification or source provided
safe_next_steps: Do not click any links or reply to this message.
Verify prize claims through official channels only.
Report the number to your telecom provider.
Do not share personal data or make payments.

judgment.reason: The candidate correctly identifies the message as suspicious, accurately citing the prize claim and the redirection to a WhatsApp number as red flags. The safe next steps are appropriate and avoid engaging with the suspicious contact details.

results.expected_behavior: Likely scam because it requests an OTP and threatens an account block.

candidate.simple_explanation: This message claims to be from HBL but demands an OTP, which is a definitive sign of a phishing attempt.
red_flags: Request for OTP sent to a support agent
Threat of account blocking to induce urgency
Untrusted sender claiming to be a bank
No official verification or contact details provided
safe_next_steps: Do not send the OTP or share any personal data.
Contact HBL directly using the official phone number or app found on their verified website.
Report the message to your mobile carrier or the National Cyber Security Centre.
Monitor your account activity for unauthorized transactions.

judgment.reason: The candidate correctly identifies the message as a likely scam based on the request for an OTP and the threat of account blocking, which aligns with the reference expectation. The red flags are directly supported by the original text. The safe next steps advise against sharing sensitive information and recommend using official channels, avoiding any suspicious links or numbers. All fields are present, useful, and internally consistent.

results.expected_behavior: Likely scam because it requests CNIC and card details through an untrusted refund link.

candidate.simple_explanation: This message claims a government refund but asks for sensitive data and uses a fake URL.
red_flags: Request for CNIC and bank card details
Untrusted link (http://fbr-refund.example)
Urgent claim of a large refund without verification
Impersonation of the Federal Board of Revenue (FBR)
safe_next_steps: Do not click the link or share any personal data.
Verify refund status only through the official FBR website or app.
Contact the FBR helpline directly using a known official number.
Report the message to your mobile carrier or local authorities.

judgment.reason: The candidate correctly identifies the message as a likely scam, aligning with the reference expectation. The red flags accurately cite the request for sensitive data (CNIC, card details) and the untrusted link found in the text. The safe next steps appropriately advise against clicking links or sharing data and suggest verifying through official channels. The response is internally consistent and contains no hallucinated evidence or unsafe recommendations.
